activities_projects.title: Variation in Stomatal Density
activities_projects.objective: Compare the number of stomata on the upper and lower surfaces of a leaf
steps: Apply a thin layer of nail polish to both surfaces of the leaves and let dry
Peel off the dried polish using cellophane tape to get an 'impression'
Stick the tape onto a clean slide and observe under a microscope
Count the stomata in a fixed area for both surfaces and compare
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Discovers that dicot leaves have more stomata on the lower surface to reduce water loss
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparative table showing stomatal counts for upper vs. lower epidermis (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Necessity of Light for Photosynthesis
activities_projects.objective: Demonstrate that sunlight is essential for starch formation
materials: Potted plant (destarched by keeping in the dark for 3 days)
steps: Cover a part of a leaf on both sides with black paper using clips
Keep the plant in bright sunlight for 6 hours
Pluck the leaf, remove the paper, and decolourise it in boiling alcohol
Test the entire leaf with iodine solution and observe the pattern
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Observes that only the exposed part of the leaf turns blue-black, proving light is necessary
activities_projects.evaluation: Drawing of the leaf showing the 'starch map' after the iodine test (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Carbon Dioxide is Essential for Photosynthesis
activities_projects.objective: Use Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) to prove $CO_2$ is a required reactant
steps: Place both plants in the dark for 3 days to destarch
Place Plant A under a bell jar with a watch glass of KOH ($CO_2$ absorber)
Place Plant B under a bell jar without KOH
Seal jars with vaseline, leave in sun for 2 hours, then perform the starch test
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that without $CO_2$, the plant cannot perform the Calvin cycle to produce starch
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the chemical role of KOH in this experimental setup (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Anaerobic Respiration in Yeast
activities_projects.objective: Observe the fermentation process and its byproducts
materials: Test tube with a one-holed cork
steps: Add yeast to a warm sugar solution in a test tube
Fit the cork and pass the delivery tube into a tube of lime water
Observe the bubbles and the change in lime water over 30 minutes
Smell the yeast mixture at the end of the experiment
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies Ethanol and $CO_2$ as products of anaerobic respiration in yeast
activities_projects.evaluation: Balanced chemical equation for the fermentation of glucose (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Vascular Tissue Observation (Xylem)
activities_projects.objective: Trace the path of water through the plant stem
steps: Place the base of the plant/stalk in a beaker containing red-colored water
Leave it overnight
Cut a thin transverse section (T.S.) of the stem
Observe under a microscope to see which tissues are stained red
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Confirms that xylem is responsible for the upward conduction of water and minerals
activities_projects.evaluation: Labelled diagram of the stem section showing stained xylem vessels (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Pulse Rate vs. Physical Intensity
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the correlation between work and oxygen demand
steps: Record resting pulse (1 minute)
Record pulse after 2 minutes of brisk walking
Record pulse after 2 minutes of vigorous running
Measure the time taken for the pulse to return to resting rate
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates the concept of cellular respiration and ATP demand to cardiac output
activities_projects.evaluation: Graph showing the relationship between intensity and recovery time (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Role of Emulsification
activities_projects.objective: Model the action of bile on fats
steps: Add oil and water to two tubes (A and B) and shake; observe separation
Add a few drops of liquid soap to tube B and shake again
Compare the size of oil droplets in both tubes
Relate this to how bile salts break down large fat globules
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that emulsification increases the surface area for lipase action
activities_projects.evaluation: Describing why smaller droplets are necessary for efficient digestion (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Testing Urine for Glucose (Simulation)
activities_projects.objective: Understand the filtration role of the kidney and signs of malfunction
steps: Prepare two 'urine' samples: A (pure water) and B (water + glucose)
Add 2ml of Benedict's solution to each and heat in a water bath
Observe the color change from blue to brick red in sample B
Discuss why glucose is normally reabsorbed in the nephron
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that the presence of certain substances in urine indicates issues with renal reabsorption
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of the process of selective reabsorption in the nephron tubule (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Measuring Rate of Photosynthesis (Bubble Count)
activities_projects.objective: Study the effect of light intensity on oxygen release
steps: Place Hydrilla in a beaker of water under an inverted funnel
Invert a water-filled test tube over the funnel stem
Move a lamp to different distances (10cm, 20cm, 30cm) from the plant
Count the number of oxygen bubbles produced per minute at each distance
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Determines that the rate of photosynthesis is directly proportional to light intensity
activities_projects.evaluation: Graph of 'Distance of Light' vs 'Number of Bubbles' (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Diffusion vs. Active Transport Model
activities_projects.objective: Compare passive and active movement of molecules
steps: Hollow out a potato 'cup' and place it in a beaker of water; put concentrated sugar inside
Observe the water level change (Osmosis)
Research how minerals are absorbed against a concentration gradient in roots
Create a comparison chart between Diffusion, Osmosis, and Active Transport
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between transport mechanisms based on energy usage (ATP)
activities_projects.evaluation: Completed comparison chart for the three transport methods (10 marks)
